zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Richard Charles Clements於大約1506年出生在英國萊希斯特斯爾卡芙特時，他的父親Sir William Robert Clements of Croft26歲，母親Sybil Isabel Llwyd25歲。他和Elizabeth Isley至少有3個兒子和3個女兒。他去世於1571年2月3日在他的家鄉享年66歲，埋葬在英國萊希斯特斯爾卡芙特。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Richard Charles Clements于大约1506年出生在英国萊希斯特斯爾卡芙特时，他的父亲Sir William Robert Clements of Croft26岁，母亲Sybil Isabel Llwyd25岁。他和Elizabeth Isley至少有3个儿子和3个女儿。他去世于1571年2月3日在他的家乡享年66岁，埋葬在英国萊希斯特斯爾卡芙特。

nameMeanings.name: Clements
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, Irish (Tyrone and Antrim), and Dutch: variant of Clement , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s. In North America, this surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. German Clementz and Slovenian patronymic Klemenc (see Klements ).

variants.name: Clemence
meanings: English and French: from the Middle English and Old French female personal name Clemence (from Latin clementia ‘mildness’).
In some cases also an Americanized form of Slovenian Klemenc (see Klements ).

variants.name: Clement
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Alcide, Alphonse, Andre, Marcel, Armand, Cecile, Christophe, Gaston, Olivier, Aime.

English, Catalan, German, Flemish, and Dutch; French and Walloon (Clément): from the Latin personal name Clemens meaning ‘merciful’ (genitive Clementis). This achieved popularity firstly through having been borne by an early Christian saint who was a disciple of Saint Paul, and later because it was selected as a symbolic name by a number of early popes. There has also been some confusion with the personal name Clemence (from Latin Clementia, meaning ‘mercy’, an abstract noun derived from the adjective; in part a masculine name from Latin Clementius, a later derivative of Clemens). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, especially Italian Clemente , and also their derivatives.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

variants.name: George
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

nameMeanings.name: Charles
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From a Germanic word, karl, meaning ‘free man’, akin to Old English ceorl ‘man’. The name, Latin form Carolus, owed its popularity in medieval Europe to the Frankish leader Charlemagne ( ?742–814 ), who in 800 established himself as Holy Roman Emperor. His name (Latin Carolus Magnus) means ‘Charles the Great’. Carolus—or Karl, the German form—was a common name among Frankish leaders, including Charlemagne's grandfather Charles Martel ( 688–741 ). Charles is the French form. The name occurs occasionally in medieval Britain as Karolus or Carolus; it had a certain vogue in West Yorkshire from the 1400s, particularly among gentry families. The form Charles was chosen by Mary Queen of Scots ( 1542–87 ), who had been brought up in France, for her son, Charles James ( 1566–1625 ), who became King James VI of Scotland and, from 1603 , James I of England. His son and grandson both reigned as King Charles , and the name thus became established in the 17th century both in the Stuart royal house and among English and Scottish supporters of the Stuart monarchy. In the 18th century it was to some extent favoured, along with James , by Jacobites, supporters of the exiled Stuarts, opposed to the Hanoverian monarchy, especially in the Highlands of Scotland. In the 19th century the popularity of the name was further enhanced by romanticization of the story of ‘Bonnie Prince Charlie’, leader of the 1745 rebellion.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Richard Charles Clements was born about 1506, in Croft, Leicestershire, England, his father, Sir William Robert Clements of Croft, was 26 and his mother, Sybil Isabel Llwyd, was 25. He had at least 3 sons and 3 daughters with Elizabeth Isley. He died on 3 February 1571, in his hometown, at the age of 66, and was buried in Croft, Leicestershire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Richard Charles Clements est né environ 1506, à Croft, Leicestershire, Angleterre, son père, Sir William Robert Clements of Croft, avait 26 ans et sa mère, Sybil Isabel Llwyd, avait 25 ans. Il a eu au moins 3 fils et 3 filles avec Elizabeth Isley. Il est décédé le 3 février 1571, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 66 ans, et a été enterré à Croft, Leicestershire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Richard Charles Clements um 1506 in Croft, Leicestershire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Sir William Robert Clements of Croft 26 und seine Mutter Sybil Isabel Llwyd 25. Er hatte mit Elizabeth Isley mindestens 3 Söhne und 3 Töchter. Er starb am 3. Februar 1571 im Alter von 66 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort und wurde in Croft, Leicestershire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Richard Charles Clements è nato il circa 1506 nel Croft, Leicestershire, England, il suo padre, Sir William Robert Clements of Croft, aveva 26 anni e il suo madre, Sybil Isabel Llwyd, aveva 25 anni. Lui avuto almeno 3 figli e 3 figlie con Elizabeth Isley. Morì il 3 febbraio 1571, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 66 anni, e fu sepolto a Croft, Leicestershire, England.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Charles Clementsが約1506年にイギリスレアイセステルシュアイアクロフトで生まれた時，父Sir William Robert Clements of Croftは26歳，母Sybil Isabel Llwydは25歳であった。彼はElizabeth Isleyとの間に少なくとも3人の息子と3人の娘をもうけた。彼は1571年2月3日に故郷で66歳で死去し，イギリスレアイセステルシュアイアクロフトにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Charles Clements(이)가 대략 1506년 영국 레이스스터실르 크로프트에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir William Robert Clements of Croft(은)는 26세였고, 그의 어머니 Sybil Isabel Llwyd(은)는 25세였다. 그는 Elizabeth Isley(와)과 적어도 세 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1571년 2월 3일 그의 고향에서 66세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 레이스스터실르 크로프트에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Richard Charles Clements nasceu aproximadamente 1506, em Croft, Leicestershire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Sir William Robert Clements of Croft, tinha 26 anos e sua mãe, Sybil Isabel Llwyd, tinha 25 anos. Ele teve pelo menos 3 filhos e 3 filhas com Elizabeth Isley. Ele faleceu em 3 de fevereiro de 1571, em sua cidade natal, com 66 anos, e foi sepultado em Croft, Leicestershire, Inglaterra.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Richard Charles Clements родился около 1506 г. в местности крофт, лисестиршир, Англия, его отцу по имени Sir William Robert Clements of Croft было 26 лет, а матери, которую звали Sybil Isabel Llwyd, 25. У него было по крайней мере 3 сына и 3 дочери от Elizabeth Isley. Он умер 3 февраля 1571 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 66 лет и был похоронен в местности крофт, лисестиршир, Англия.
